item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0) due to the presence of "NC" in the license. As such, it has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Surprising thing here is a: they actually let her go and
b: practically no expungements at all.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a sweetheart.. I want to hug her. +1, I cried a bit.

Yaaay! I'm super stoked you guys liked this enough to bring it to good ol' triple digits. So now… as a thank you… I present bonus content.

+ Open Letter To Dr. Wey
- Close Letter To Dr. Wey

Dear Dr. Wey,

When I asked to see you before my final release the security people told me you were busy, or gone. I can't tell if they were lying or not, but they told me they could deliver a letter, which I guess is better than nothing.

Just wanted to say thank you. For the countless hours you spent sitting across from me, discussing myself. It couldn't have been fun to listen to a whiny teenager complain no a weekly basis. But I can't express how grateful I am you did it anyways. I don't know what would've become of me if they just kept me in that cell.

They told me I won't remember you after they let me go. I hope that's not the case. I want to come back and say thank you in person. I really do. After sitting across from you week after week it feels awkward to express myself in a letter like this. But, on the off chance you need someone to hear out your problems, I don't care if I remember you or not, you can come and talk to me.

Not that I know where I'll be, but you work for a big secret organization with secrets around every corner. I think you're able to figure it out.

- Erica Stross

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great, but I was really hoping she'd join her new family, the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3511 had turned eighteen that day.

Spoilers
SPOILERS

It is implied this effect is given to her by her parents, if only because her parents seem very weird… so why? Well, if she's supposed to be so special, maybe she got into a lot of fights and that scared her parents. They want her to be safe, of course! They're parents, after all! So, doing what they do best, or maybe the only thing they know how to do, they made her untouchable. No more bullies, no more possible pedophiles, no whaps or whips from teachers or peers. No one would ever harm our child again.

And maybe if it was to protect a child, then it would follow that she would lose it when she got to adulthood. You're an adult now, you can protect yourself! So, turns 18, ability turns off. She can handle her own responsibilities now, for as far as her parents are concerned.

There was probably a way to write that to make the reveal more "oooh", but like, I just wanted to respond.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oof, bad parenting in skip form. Really well-written, and the characters are very nicely developed. Definite +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aw, well this was heartwarming. I do feel like more detail could be put into the actual description (was a little confused by it at the start), but other than that I really liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dislike how small of an anomaly this was but it was still enjoyable to read.

There are quite some questions though. Were the Fifthist really her parents? Did they give her the anomaly when she was injured? Will they return to collect their little starfish? Why did her anomaly went away?

It's quite an interesting read and I love the imagery for the end of it. Good job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
3511

Illuminatis confirmed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We must protect the starfish

Fifthist, I assume?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Poor thing should've been introduced to 999…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty neutral on the skip itself, and I must say that the old version of this skip was much better, in my opinion. I have no quarrels with this version, through, so novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… I'll be honest when I first wrote those interviews I totally forgot fifthism was a thing. But when I showed it to people later, they said it made sense, and so I kept it.

Or the short answer: yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is about as close to a happy ending as I've seen here in a while. I'm hoping the Foundation finds the parents before they find her.

What did they do to their little starfish? WHY did they do it? We may never know.

Exceptionally well written and emotionally resonant.

I was TERRIFIED when I saw the last log say she was "neutralized". not sure if that was intentionally vague wording, but damn did that throw me on an emotional rollercoaster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, actually, I must have bleeped over the relevant section. :B Just some lip-service that they attempted was all I was looking for, and it was already there! Ignore me! :V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I always like happy endings and, maybe its just me, but they feel kind of rare on this site.

As for the parents, The Foundation is looking for them, but I didn't want to focus on it too much, because it'd take some focus away from Erica.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, what a feel-good skip. :D

At the mention of "starfish", I started wondering if this wasn't Fifthist, but nothing seemed to come of that. No investigation into her parents, after the revelation that they likely made her anomalous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only real complaint is that she talks like she’s British despite supposedly being an American. Same for the guy. American teenagers don’t say mental or picnic in those contexts.

Other than that, it was entertaining and heartfelt. It’s always nice to see the Foundation’s human side.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll admit you almost lost me in the beginning with such a tiny anomaly, but I'm glad I stuck around. I like the happy ending, and I really like the implication that the parents are the true anomaly here. Might we assume a Fifthist connection with the 'Starfish' nickname?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get how you'd see the ending like that. I was going for a subversion of expectations. That way the reversal would provide a breath of fresh air as well as a different kind of climax. Either way, I'm happy you liked the rest of it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought I recognised this when reading. Man this has changed a lot.

I have to say, not a fan of the ending, I get she's turned 18 but it just seems a bit of anti climax. I still enjoyed it though so +1.

item.title: Re: SCP-3511
item.content:encoded:
I really liked the narrative behind this scip. I enjoyed reading all of the logs, as they were all interesting.

For some reason, my favorite part of all of this was probably Dr. Wey himself. I think it was because he and SCP-3511 were genuine characters, not just another faceless individual of the Foundation like you usually see in transcripts.

They both had their own little story develop over time with the logs, and Dr. Wey even made a relationship with SCP-2511.

It was sad, but the very ending made me happy. Now I want to give her a hug. :p

+1. Keep it up!

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Here it is! Yet another skip!

I'd like to thank the many many many people who read various versions of this: DarkStuff, TheGreatSea, Weryllium, psul, DrMagnus, stormbreath, DrChandra, taylor_itkin, Tanhony, GibberingEloquence and probably many others who I have forgotten (pm me and I'll add you).

Image Credit: https://dod.defense.gov/News/Special-Reports/Hispanic-Heritage-archive/

Now go give someone a hug.

If you liked this article, then you can check out more of my work here!
